sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Supplemental oxygen is a treatment for hypoxaemia and selected high-risk clinical states. In prehospital care, oxygen should be used purposefully and titrated to the clinical picture and local target range, rather than applied reflexively at maximum flow.

sections.title: Delivery Devices
sections.content:
- Choose the device that matches the patient's severity, tolerance, likely pathology, and required FiO2
- Common options include nasal cannulae, simple face mask, non-rebreather mask, Venturi systems, and bag-valve-mask ventilation with oxygen
- Escalate the device or flow when hypoxia or work of breathing is not improving, and de-escalate when excessive oxygen is unnecessary

sections.title: Targets & Clinical Use
sections.content:
- For most acutely unwell adults, oxygen is guided by hypoxia, work of breathing, and the clinical syndrome
- Patients at risk of chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure may need a lower target range according to local protocol
- Carbon monoxide poisoning, major trauma, peri-arrest states, and severe shock may justify high-concentration oxygen despite a misleading or initially normal SpO2
- Avoid letting a normal saturation distract from severe hypoventilation or exhaustion

sections.title: Key Points
sections.content:
- Oxygen is a treatment, not just a routine observation response
- Always document device, flow rate, indication, and response
- Reassess saturation, respiratory effort, mental state, and tolerance after changes
- Pulse oximetry limitations still apply when deciding how much oxygen the patient needs
